sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=30, col=10).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 50, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'DOWN': 'Moves blue player 4 rows down, decreases timer by 2 pixels; stopped at row 44', 'LEFT': 'Moves blue player 4 cols left, decreases timer by 2 pixels', 'UP': 'Moves blue player 4 rows up, decreases timer by 2 pixels; stops at row 12', 'RIGHT': 'Moves blue player 4 cols right, decreases timer by 2 pixels', 'SPACE': 'Decreases timer by 2, no movement or other effect VERIFIED(step 37)', 'MOUSE': 'Decreases timer by 2, no special effect on blocks or containers VERIFIED(step 33-36)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'N_strip_top': 'Green timer bar at row 0, starts at 64 pixels', 'O_background': 'Orange main background', 'c_block': 'Charcoal block at rows 1-3, cols 36-39 (3x4)', 'M_block': 'Magenta block at rows 4-7, cols 36-39 (4x4)', 'b_rectangle': 'Blue 4x20 player rectangle, moves with arrow keys', 'Y_shapes': 'Two U-shaped yellow containers at bottom rows 52-59, left at cols 16-27, right at cols 40-51', 'w_strip_bottom': 'Light gray floor rows 60-63'}
- HUD map: {'top_green_strip': 'Timer - decreases 2 per action, game over at 0'}
- Win pattern: {'game_over': 'Timer reaches 0 causes game over and reset VERIFIED(step 32)'}
- Level log: {'level_1_attempt_1': 'Failed - timer ran out after 31 actions exploring movement'}
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 49 actions; a strong score needs about 39 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
MOUSE on background also just decreased timer. I've now tried MOUSE on every type of object and nothing does anything special. Let me step back and think about what I might be fundamentally misunderstanding.
Let me re-examine the grid more carefully. Maybe I'm wrong about the segmentation. Let me look at the current frame's segmentation to see if there are any objects I missed.
Actually, let me reconsider the problem from scratch. The game has:
1. A timer bar (green) at top
2. Two blocks (charcoal and magenta) near top-right
3. A blue player rectangle
4. Two yellow U-shaped containers at bottom
5. A gray floor
What if the goal is simply to MOVE the player into the containers? Like, the player needs to fit inside one of the U-shaped containers?
The containers are at rows 52-59, cols 16-27 (left) and cols 40-51 (right). The player is 4 rows tall and 20 cols wide. The left container is 12 cols wide (16-27), and the right container is also 12 cols wide (40-51). The player is 20 cols wide, so it doesn't fit inside either container.
Wait, but the containers are U-shaped with openings. The left container has an opening at cols 20-23 (4 cols wide). The player is 20 cols wide, so it can't fit through a 4-col opening.
Hmm, what if the player can SHRINK? Or what if there's a mechanic I haven't discovered?
Let me look at the segmentation more carefully. Maybe there are objects I'm not seeing. Let me check if there are any small objects or hidden elements.
Actually, let me reconsider the block positions. The blocks are at cols 36-39. The left container opening is at cols 20-23 and the right container opening is at cols 44-47. The blocks don't align with either container.
Wait - what if the blocks can be moved? What if the player can PUSH the blocks? If the player moves RIGHT, it moves to cols 16-35, then 20-39, then 24-43, etc. When the player is at cols 20-39, it overlaps with the blocks at cols 36-39. But the player is at rows 16-19 and the blocks are at rows 1-7, so they don't overlap vertically.
What if the player can push blocks VERTICALLY? Like, if the player moves UP and reaches the blocks, it pushes them up? But the player stops at row 12, which is still below the blocks at rows 1-7.
Let me try a completely different hypothesis. What if the game is about the player "catching" falling blocks? Like, the blocks fall from the top and the player needs to be in the right position to catch them? But the blocks don't fall on their own.
What if SPACE makes the blocks fall? I tried SPACE but nothing happened. But maybe I need to be in the right position first?
Let me try: move player to be under the blocks (same columns), then SPACE. The blocks are at cols 36-39. The player is 20 wide. If player is at cols 20-39, it overlaps horizontally with blocks. Let me try RIGHT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Wait, I think I already tried this. Let me check the history... I moved RIGHT twice (player at cols 20-39) and then SPACE, but nothing happened.
OK let me try something I haven't tried: what if I need to move the player to the BOTTOM of the screen, into the container area, and then press SPACE? The player can go DOWN to row 44. The containers are at rows 52-59. So the player can't reach the containers.
Hmm, what if the player CAN go lower but I haven't tried enough DOWN moves? Let me check: player starts at row 16. Each DOWN moves 4 rows. So: 16 -> 20 -> 24 -> 28 -> 32 -> 36 -> 40 -> 44. That's 7 DOWN moves. I said the player stopped at row 44. Let me verify this by trying more DOWN moves.
Actually, I think I should just try a bunch of DOWN moves and see if the player can go lower. Maybe it can go all the way to the containers!

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me verify player's actual position and try to go lower
# Check current player position from segmentation
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'b':
        # Blue player
        boundary = node['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in boundary]
        cols = [p[1] for p in boundary]
        print(f"Player: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
        player_top = min(rows)
        player_left = min(cols)

# Try moving DOWN many times to see if player can reach containers
# Player is at row 16, each DOWN = 4 rows
# 16 -> 20 -> 24 -> 28 -> 32 -> 36 -> 40 -> 44 -> 48 -> 52 -> 56
# If player can reach row 52, it would be at container level
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"DOWN": "Moves blue player 4 rows down, decreases timer by 2", "LEFT": "Moves blue player 4 cols left, decreases timer by 2", "UP": "Moves blue player 4 rows up, decreases timer by 2; stops at row 12", "RIGHT": "Moves blue player 4 cols right, decreases timer by 2", "SPACE": "Decreases timer by 2, no movement", "MOUSE": "Decreases timer by 2, no special effect"}, "object_taxonomy": {"N_strip_top": "Green timer bar at row 0", "O_background": "Orange main background", "c_block": "Charcoal block rows 1-3, cols 36-39", "M_block": "Magenta block rows 4-7, cols 36-39", "b_rectangle": "Blue 4x20 player rectangle", "Y_shapes": "Two U-shaped yellow containers at bottom", "w_strip_bottom": "Light gray floor"}, "hud_map": {"top_green_strip": "Timer - decreases 2 per action"}, "win_pattern": {"game_over": "Timer reaches 0 causes game over"}, "level_log": {"level_1": "Multiple failed attempts"}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Timer=58. Player at rows 16-19, cols 12-31.", "goal_model": "Testing if player can reach container level by going DOWN many times", "recent_findings": ["MOUSE on background: no special effect"], "open_questions": ["Can player go lower than row 44?"], "current_plan": "Move DOWN 7 times to test lower boundary", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["MOUSE on blocks/containers/player/timer/background", "SPACE at various positions", "Overlapping player with blocks"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
